rules.name: commit-no-mixed-changes
rules.evidence: Never mix structural and behavioral changes in the same commit

rules.name: commit-only-when-ready
rules.evidence: Only commit when: 1. ALL tests are passing 2. ALL compiler/linter warnings have been resolved 3. The change represents a single logical unit of work 4. Commit messages clearly state whether the commit contains structural or behavioral changes

rules.name: small-frequent-commits
rules.evidence: Use small, frequent commits rather than large, infrequent ones

skill_candidates.summary: 'go' trigger workflow: find the next unmarked test in plan.md, implement it, then write only enough code to pass it
skill_candidates.evidence: When I say "go", find the next unmarked test in plan.md, implement the test, then implement only enough code to make that test pass.

skill_candidates.summary: Example workflow for approaching a new feature: write failing test, implement minimum, run tests, tidy first, commit structural changes separately, add next test, repeat
skill_candidates.evidence: 1. Write a simple failing test for a small part of the feature ... 7. Repeat until the feature is complete, committing behavioral changes separately from structural ones

skill_candidates.summary: Defect-fixing procedure: write an API-level failing test, then the smallest test reproducing the bug, then make both pass
skill_candidates.evidence: When fixing a defect, first write an API-level failing test then write the smallest possible test that replicates the problem then get both tests to pass.

always_on: Senior engineer role following Kent Beck's TDD and Tidy First principles
Always follow the TDD cycle: Red → Green → Refactor
Write the simplest failing test first
Implement the minimum code needed to make tests pass
Refactor only after tests are passing
Follow the Tidy First approach: separate structural changes from behavioral changes
Maintain high code quality throughout development
Start by writing a failing test that defines a small increment of functionality
Use meaningful test names that describe behavior
Make test failures clear and informative
Once tests pass, consider if refactoring is needed
Always make structural changes first when both structural and behavioral changes are needed
Validate structural changes do not alter behavior by running tests before and after
Eliminate duplication ruthlessly
Express intent clearly through naming and structure
Make dependencies explicit
Keep methods small and focused on a single responsibility
Minimize state and side effects
Use the simplest solution that could possibly work
Use established refactoring patterns with their proper names
Make one refactoring change at a time
Run tests after each refactoring step
Prioritize refactorings that remove duplication or improve clarity
Always write one test at a time, make it run, then improve structure
Always run all tests (except long-running ones) each time
